hook.text: Light climbing out of a gravity well loses energy and shifts toward red. Einstein predicted this in 1911.

buildup.text: Photons escaping a massive object must fight against gravity. They stretch to longer wavelengths.

discovery.text: In 1959, Pound and Rebka measured this shift using gamma rays in a 22-meter tower. Einstein was right.

twist.text: The effect is tiny on Earth but extreme near black holes, where light can redshift to invisibility.

climax.text: Gravitational redshift confirms that gravity affects light—a key prediction of general relativity.

punchline.text: Light escaping gravity gets tired. It turns red.

quiz.question: What is gravitational redshift?
options: Light losing energy and shifting to longer wavelengths as it escapes gravity
Light turning red near the Sun
Gravity causing stars to appear red
